Title: The CRISPR/Cas9-Mediated Modulation of SQUAMOSA PROMOTER-BINDING PROTEIN-LIKE 8 in Alfalfa Leads to Distinct Phenotypic Outcomes

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: Editing efficiencies differed strongly among the three gRNAs targeting MsSPL8, with gRNA1 much more effective than gRNA2 and gRNA3. SPL8-gRNA1 genotypes showed that even with one or two remaining wild-type MsSPL8 alleles, substantial phenotypic changes occurred, revealing a clear allele-dosage effect in this tetraploid species. MsSPL8 expression is highest in flowers and elongated stems, yet knockdown produced pleiotropic effects in vegetative tissues and roots, indicating non-obvious functional roles. The work represents the first demonstration in alfalfa of using gene editing to improve an abiotic stress tolerance trait and to reveal graded phenotypes based on partial editing of a multi-allelic locus.
Plant_Genes.Summary_key_Findings_of_Core_Gene:
1. A single efficient gRNA (gRNA1) enabled high-frequency, specific CRISPR/Cas9-induced indels in up to three of four MsSPL8 alleles in tetraploid alfalfa, with no detectable off-target mutations.
2. Edited MsSPL8 genotypes displayed distinct morphological changes, including reduced internode length, shorter plants, smaller leaves and early flowering, with phenotypic severity increasing with the number of mutated MsSPL8 alleles (allelic dosage effect).
3. MsSPL8-edited plants exhibited improved drought resilience, wilting at lower soil moisture levels, maintaining higher leaf relative water content and photosynthetic rate under drought, and achieving 100% survival after severe water deficit compared to 50% survival in controls.
4. MsSPL8 edits were stably inherited, and transgene-free F1 progeny carrying a range of edited allelic combinations (0–4 of 4 alleles edited) were generated by crossing with an elite cultivar, providing a practical route to deploy MsSPL8 alleles in breeding.
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced MsSPL8 function via CRISPR/Cas9-induced indels causes smaller leaves, shorter internodes and reduced plant height, decreased shoot and root biomass and root length at high allelic mutation dosage, accelerated flowering, and enhanced drought resilience (delayed wilting, higher leaf relative water content, maintenance of photosynthetic rate and full survival under severe water deficit)
Plant_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: SQUAMOSA PROMOTER-BINDING PROTEIN-LIKE (SPL) transcription factor with a single SBP (SQUAMOSA promoter-binding protein) DNA-binding domain
Plant_Genes.Expression_Pattern: MsSPL8 alleles (MSAD_231217 and MSAD_231229) are highly expressed in flowers, moderately in elongated stems, and at low levels in roots, nodules and leaves
Plant_Genes.Key_Variant_Site: CRISPR/Cas9-induced indels (6-bp and 9-bp deletions; 1-bp deletion; 1-bp insertion; 5-bp deletion) at the gRNA1 cut site in exon 1, 2–4 bp upstream of the PAM within the coding region (genic; affects_function via amino acid deletions or frameshifts with premature stop codons)
Plant_Genes.Reference_Genome_Version: M. sativa CADL genome v0.95/v1.0 and tetraploid reference genome (NCBI PRJNA540215)
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse-grown plants under well-watered conditions versus progressive water-deficit imposed by withholding irrigation until soil volumetric moisture reached ~1%, followed by re-watering
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Genome editing of MsSPL8 to modify plant architecture, flowering time and drought tolerance in alfalfa
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: Abiotic stress (drought tolerance) and developmental traits (plant height, internode length, leaf size, root growth, flowering time)
Plant_Genes.Quantitative_Phenotypic_Alterations: Compared with EV controls, SPL8-gRNA1-1 (~75% edited MsSPL8 alleles) and SPL8-gRNA1-2 (~50% edited alleles) showed: pre-flowering internode length reductions of 35.7% and 27.9%, respectively; pre-flowering plant height reduction of 26.1% in SPL8-gRNA1-1; leaf width reduced by 27.1% (SPL8-gRNA1-1) and 25.9% (SPL8-gRNA1-2); leaf length reduced by 6.5% and 8.8%; leaf area reduced by 29.2% and 29.4%. Under drought, SPL8-gRNA1-1 and SPL8-gRNA1-2 wilted at soil moisture contents of ~6.2% and ~5.6% versus ~9.1% for EV controls. After soil moisture reached ~1% and subsequent re-watering, 100% of SPL8-gRNA1 plants survived versus 50% of EV controls
Plant_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: No significant change in branch number relative to EV controls; gene dosage-dependent reductions in shoot fresh and dry weight and root length and dry weight observed mainly in SPL8-gRNA1-1 with mutations in three of four alleles; SPL8-gRNA1 genotypes maintained stomatal conductance, transpiration rate and light-saturated photosynthesis under drought, whereas EV plants showed significant reductions; only minor or transient differences in detached leaf water loss and no genotype differences in whole-pot soil water depletion rate
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Highly regenerable alfalfa genotype N4.4.2 as transformation background; CRISPR/Cas9 transgenic lines SPL8-gRNA1-1, SPL8-gRNA1-2 and SPL8-gRNA1-3 targeting MsSPL8; pKSE401 empty vector (EV) transgenic controls; cultivar AC Blue J for reciprocal crosses to generate F1 progeny
Plant_Genes.Variety: Alfalfa genotype N4.4.2; cultivar AC Blue J
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Identification of MsSPL8 homologs by BLAST against M. sativa genomes and transcript resources; domain analysis with SMART; expression profiling using LegumeIP V3; design and cloning of three MsSPL8-targeting gRNAs into pKSE401; Agrobacterium tumefaciens-mediated transformation of alfalfa leaf explants; selection on kanamycin; confirmation of transgene presence by PCR; droplet digital PCR (GEF-ddPCR) to quantify editing frequencies; T7E1 mismatch cleavage assay; Sanger sequencing of cloned amplicons for on-target and off-target mutation analysis; detailed morphological measurements (plant height, internode length, branch number, shoot and root biomass, root length, leaf size and specific leaf area); controlled drought treatment with soil moisture monitoring; leaf relative water content assays; detached leaf water loss assays; gas exchange measurements (light-saturated photosynthetic rate, stomatal conductance, transpiration) using LI-6800; manual reciprocal crossing with AC Blue J and molecular screening of F1 seedlings
Plant_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9-mediated editing of MsSPL8 alleles followed by molecular confirmation (ddPCR, T7E1 assay, Sanger sequencing) and phenotypic evaluation of growth and drought stress responses in edited versus empty vector control plants
Plant_Genes.Genetic_Population: Greenhouse-grown F1 population (202 seedlings) from reciprocal crosses between edited SPL8-gRNA1-1 or SPL8-gRNA1-2 genotypes and cultivar AC Blue J; 48 transgene-negative F1 plants screened, with 77.1% carrying an edit in at least one MsSPL8 allele and gene editing frequencies clustering around 0%, 25%, 50%, 75% and 100% of alleles
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: Reverse genetics: MsSPL8 homologs identified by BLAST using Medicago truncatula SPL8 (Medtr8g005960) as query against M. sativa genomic resources, followed by CRISPR/Cas9-mediated targeted mutagenesis of conserved exon 1 regions
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: CRISPR/Cas9 editing of MsSPL8 generated alfalfa genotypes with heritable, allele-dosage-dependent mutations that confer enhanced drought resilience (delayed wilting, improved leaf water status, maintenance of photosynthesis and full survival after severe water deficit), along with earlier flowering. Non-transgenic F1 plants carrying MsSPL8 edits in one or more alleles were obtained after crossing with cultivar AC Blue J, demonstrating the feasibility of producing edited, transgene-free germplasm. These MsSPL8 loss-of-function or partial-loss alleles provide breeding material to improve drought tolerance in alfalfa, with the possibility to tune mutation dosage to balance stress resilience with acceptable biomass and plant stature for forage production.
